question: What is being discussed about Chapter 168 at the Historic District Commission meeting?
expected_answer: Suggested updates to Chapter 168 Historic Buildings and Districts, including the City's Demolition Delay procedures, will be presented and discussed as a Citizen Presentation & Discussion.
source_title: Historic Districts Commission Meeting
actual_answer:
According to the April 29, 2026 Historic Districts Commission meeting agenda, there will be a **citizen presentation and discussion on suggested updates to Chapter 168 Historic Buildings and Districts, including the City's Demolition Delay procedures**.

Demolition Delay is a process that gives the city time to evaluate whether a historic building should be preserved before it can be demolished. The discussion will focus on potential changes to how this process works in Beverly's historic district regulations.

The meeting is scheduled for Wednesday, April 29, 2026 at 7:15 PM in the City Council Chambers at Beverly City Hall, 191 Cabot Street. You can find more details at https://www.beverlyma.gov/Calendar.aspx?EID=3521&month=4&year=2026&day=23&calType=0

question: What improvements are planned for Glover Wharf?
expected_answer: Planned improvements include installing a dinghy bar and additional fishing-related and dinghy tie-up signage, adding security gates to the commercial float, upgrading electricity on the recreational float, and installing a webcam on the transient float.
source_title: Harbor Management Authority Meeting
actual_answer:
Based on the Harbor Management Authority meeting agenda from April 15, 2026, the following improvements are planned for Glover Wharf:

**Facilities and Infrastructure:**
- Install a dinghy bar and additional fishing-related and dinghy tie-up signage
- Add security gates to the commercial float (a CW White add-on)
- Complete phase 2 of the Hoist Pier
- Upgrade electricity to the recreational float
- Install a webcam, primarily for the transient float
- Evaluate ice machine options

**Customer Service:**
- Update the commercial marina annual rate (to begin July 1)
- Post commercial rates to the website
- Send rate letters to commercial customers

You can view the full agenda at https://www.beverlyma.gov/Calendar.aspx?EID=1503&month=9&year=2024&day=5&calType=0.

question: What topics will be discussed at the Stormwater Committee meeting?
expected_answer: The meeting will cover program status updates including ordinances and regulations, dry and wet weather updates, IDDE sampling, city asset inspections and maintenance, private development inspections, LID guidelines, GIS development, Year 7 objectives, and budget.
source_title: Stormwater Committee Meeting
actual_answer:
Based on the agenda for the Stormwater Committee meeting held on November 26, 2024, the following topics were discussed:

**Program Status Updates** including ordinances, rules, and regulations

**Dry Weather and Wet Weather Updates** on stormwater conditions

**IDDE Sampling Update** (IDDE stands for Illicit Discharge Detection and Elimination, a program to identify and stop illegal connections to stormwater systems)

**City Asset Inspections and Maintenance** of stormwater infrastructure

**Private Development Inspections and Maintenance** related to stormwater systems

**LID Guidelines** (LID refers to Low Impact Development, techniques that manage stormwater on-site)

**City Asset LID Retrofit** projects

**GIS Development** for mapping and tracking stormwater assets

**Year 7 Objectives** for the stormwater program

**Budget** matters for the program

The meeting also included time for **conclusions and questions**.

You can view the full agenda at: https://www.beverlyma.gov/Calendar.aspx?EID=2895&month=11&year=2024&day=5&calType=0

question: What is the Beverly Housing Rehabilitation Program and when will the city request funding for it?
expected_answer: The Beverly Housing Rehabilitation Program (BHRP) is a program that will be funded through Massachusetts Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) Program Community Development Fund 2025 funds. The City of Beverly, through the Department of Planning & Development, will request the release of these funds on or about May 1, 2026.
source_title: Notice of Intent to Request Release of Funds
actual_answer:
The **Beverly Housing Rehabilitation Program (BHRP)** is a city initiative using Massachusetts Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) funds to rehabilitate homes in Beverly. The program will support rehabilitation work for approximately 14 to 15 owner-occupied homes, primarily one- to four-family properties.

The city requested **$850,000** through the CDBG Program. The plan is to contract with an existing organization experienced in administering similar programs, while the city handles grant management and oversight. The grant is a one-year award, though the program typically allows an 18-month period for completion.

Regarding timing, the city will request the release of these funds **on or about May 1, 2026**. The program will kick off sometime after that.

For more details, visit **www.beverlyma.gov/CDBG**, where you can find the full "Notice of Intent to Request Release of Funds" document. You can also view the Environmental Review Record at the Beverly Planning & Development Department, 191 Cabot Street during business hours.

question: Who is the contractor installing the new water meters in Beverly?
expected_answer: The contractor is Hydro Utilities. You will receive an official letter from them with instructions and program details when they work in your area.
source_title: Info For City Wide Water Meter Change-Out Program
actual_answer:
According to the news announcement from February 12, 2026, the contractor installing the new water meters in Beverly is **Hydro Utilities**. 

The installation is mandatory and provided at no cost to water customers. You can schedule your meter changeout appointment with Hydro Utilities either online at beverlymeters.com or by calling them toll-free at 1-877-884-9376. You'll need your Work Order # and Service # from the notice letter to schedule.

More details are available at: https://www.beverlyma.gov/m/newsflash/Home/Detail/362

question: Will I have to pay for the new water meter installation?
expected_answer: No, the meter and reading device are supplied and installed at no cost to the water customer.
source_title: Info For City Wide Water Meter Change-Out Program
actual_answer:
No, you will not have to pay for the new water meter installation. According to the city's announcement about the water meter change-out program, the installation is mandatory, but the meter and reading device are supplied and installed at no cost to water customers.

You will receive an official letter from Hydro Utilities (the city's installation contractor) with instructions and program details when the contractor is working in your area. You are responsible for scheduling your appointment within one week of receiving the notice by visiting beverlymeters.com or calling Hydro Utilities toll-free at 1-877-884-9376.

For more details, see: https://www.beverlyma.gov/m/newsflash/Home/Detail/362

question: What is the Beverly City Hall project about?
expected_answer: The project involves renovation and expansion of Beverly City Hall, with updates being provided by the City Council.
source_title: Beverly City Hall Renovation and Expansion  City Council Project Update  January 20, 2026
actual_answer:
The Beverly City Hall project is a major renovation and expansion of City Hall and the former police station building, representing the first significant renovation of City Hall since 1935.

Here are the key details:

**Scope of Work**

The project involves renovating the existing City Hall building and demolishing the former police station annex, which will be replaced with a new two-story addition on the existing foundation. The work includes installing a second ADA-compliant elevator, adding a second entrance from the parking lot, and making the entire facility fully accessible to people with disabilities. The project will also consolidate city operations, bringing back three departments—Public Health, Inspectional Services, and the Clerk's office—that have been located elsewhere.

**Budget and Timeline**

The construction budget is approximately $21 million, with total project costs including soft costs (architectural design, project management, and contingencies) reaching around $25.9 million. The City Council authorized a loan of $25,895,000 in January 2026 to fund the project.

Originally, city staff were planned to vacate City Hall by April 2025, with construction running 18–19 months from May 2025 through the end of 2026. The city will temporarily use the Family Dollar building to relocate operations during construction.

**Current Status**

Yes, City Hall remains open to the public during the renovation, though some services may experience delays or temporary relocations.

For more details, see the January 20, 2026 City Council meeting minutes: https://www.beverlyma.gov/AgendaCenter/ViewFile/Minutes/_01202026-2598

question: Did Beverly update its immigration policy for police?
expected_answer: Yes, Police Chief LeLacheur updated the Beverly Police Department Immigration Status and Detainer Policy in 2026, building on the previous 2017 policy.
source_title: Joint Proclamation: Immigration and the Values of the City of Beverly
actual_answer:
Yes, Beverly updated its immigration policy for police. In February 2026, Mayor Mike Cahill, in consultation with Police Chief LeLacheur and other city leaders, issued a joint proclamation reaffirming the community's commitment to protecting all residents, including immigrants. Chief LeLacheur also updated the **Beverly Police Department Immigration Status and Detainer Policy** as part of this effort.

You can find the updated policy and the mayor's declaration at:
https://www.beverlyma.gov/m/newsflash/Home/Detail/363

The proclamation emphasizes keeping all Beverly residents—particularly immigrant residents—and all police personnel safe in the community.

question: Why is there a new Public Works Administration unit in the FY24 budget?
expected_answer: It's a newly restructured unit that consolidates central support administrators and staff from the Highway Division and Engineering Department into a single unit for better coordination across all Public Services divisions. There is no change to the total number of positions.
source_title: Beverly MA FY2024 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 205
actual_answer:
According to the FY24 budget, the **Public Works Administration unit** was created as a newly restructured unit within the Public Services Department. 

The reason for this restructuring was organizational efficiency: it consolidated various positions that had previously been scattered across the Highway Division and the Engineering Department into a single, centralized unit. This consolidation places central support administrators and staff in one location rather than having them distributed across multiple other units.

The budget notes that there was **no overall reduction in staff positions** (no FTE impact) — it was purely a reorganizational change. Moving forward, this unit would administer and coordinate among all the other Department of Public Services divisions and units.

question: What are the main goals for Beverly Public Schools in FY2026?
expected_answer: The three core goals are: fostering a sense of belonging for every student and staff member, empowering educators through meaningful professional growth opportunities, and upholding a commitment to innovative practices and the highest educational standards.
source_title: Beverly MA FY2026 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 6
actual_answer:
According to the FY2026 Beverly Public Schools budget, the district has three main goals:

**Designing our Equity Continuum — Diversity, Belonging, Inclusivity, Equity:** Foster an enhanced culture of respect, inclusivity, and collaboration where all district stakeholders feel valued and empowered to make positive changes in their schools and communities.

**High Quality Professional Development:** Support all staff to develop a deep understanding of effective high impact instructional strategies and how to use them to transform student engagement and performance.

**Innovative Practices:** Beverly Public Schools will implement targeted, learner-centered experiences that allow students to apply their knowledge to authentic situations, with a focus on civics understanding to promote active and informed citizenship and to empower students to make positive contributions toward addressing climate change.

These goals align with the district's mission to maximize academic achievement and personal growth and enable all students to compete in the global economy.

question: Why did the FY2024 budget increase so much compared to last year?
expected_answer: The budget increased by $6,944,500 primarily due to non-discretionary spending requirements and modest revenue increases. The city addressed the initial imbalance by refining requested funding, pulling some capital investments into FY2023, and relying on steady revenue streams to cover cost increases.
source_title: Beverly MA FY2024 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 21
actual_answer:
Based on the FY2024 budget documents, the main reason for the significant increase was **payroll and benefits costs**. 

The budget overview states that Beverly is fundamentally a service delivery organization, and the vast majority of its costs relate to delivering those services, mainly personnel and benefits. Because all city employee contracts were current with prescribed salary adjustments scheduled for FY2024, most payroll-related budget lines required additional funding compared to FY2023.

The FY2024 budget showed a total spending increase of **$6,944,500** over the approved FY2023 expenditure level. While the documents mention there were increases and decreases across almost every budget area, the personnel costs were the primary driver of this overall growth.

The budget also highlights a specific example: the **Roads and Sidewalks transfer** was budgeted at $2.1M in FY2024 (appearing as $600K less than FY2023), but the administration recommended transferring an additional $600K from free cash reserves to bring the total to $2.7M for that purpose.

question: Why does Beverly's budget use different accounting methods than regular GAAP accounting?
expected_answer: Beverly uses budgetary basis accounting in accordance with Massachusetts state law and Department of Revenue (DOR) accounting practices. This method is required for the city's annual budget and property tax certification process, even though it departs from generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) in several ways.
source_title: Beverly MA FY2025 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 17
actual_answer:
Beverly's budget uses different accounting methods than GAAP (generally accepted accounting principles) because the city is required by Massachusetts state law to use the **budgetary basis method** of accounting. This approach is established by the Commonwealth's Department of Revenue and is the standard for municipal budgeting across the state.

The budgetary basis method differs from GAAP in four main ways:

**Property taxes.** Beverly records property taxes as revenue when they are levied, rather than when they accrue.

**Encumbrances and continuing appropriations.** These are recorded as expenditures in the budget, whereas GAAP would treat them as a reservation of fund balance.

**Fund structure.** Certain activities and transactions are presented as part of the general fund in the budget, rather than as separate funds under GAAP.

**Prior year deficits and surpluses.** These are recorded as expenditure and revenue items in the budget but have no effect on GAAP expenditures and revenues.

Using this method ensures that Beverly complies with state law under Massachusetts General Law Chapter 44, Section 32 and allows the Massachusetts Department of Revenue to approve the city's property tax rates and verify that the budget is balanced and tax levies comply with Proposition 2½ limits.

question: What is the Fire Department budget for FY2025 and how does it compare to last year?
expected_answer: The FY2025 Fire Department budget is $7,965,853, which represents a decrease of $298,309 or 3.61% compared to the prior year (FY2024).
source_title: Beverly MA FY2025 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 178
actual_answer:
Based on the FY2025 Proposed Operating Budget, the **Fire Department's total expenditures for FY2025 are $7,965,853**, which represents a **3.61% decrease compared to FY2024** (down $298,309 from the prior year).

The budget document shows a five-year comparison chart indicating the Fire Department's actual and budgeted spending from FY2021 through FY2025. Despite the overall decrease, the minutes from the Finance and Property Committee meeting on June 17, 2025 reveal that the department maintained funding for core operations including minimum staffing levels (thirteen firefighters per shift), overtime for shift coverage ($600,000 budgeted for FY2025), and training programs. Some adjustments were made to reduce building maintenance, EMS supplies, and turnout gear line items due to fiscal constraints, though the committee noted funds remained sufficient for essential operations.

For more detailed information about specific budget line items within the Fire Department budget, you can review the full budget documents at: https://www.beverlyma.gov/AgendaCenter/ViewFile/Minutes/_06172025-2307

question: How far ahead does Beverly's capital plan look?
expected_answer: The plan covers a five-year period as required by the Beverly City Charter, but also includes projects that may need attention within the next ten years to support long-term financial and service planning.
source_title: Beverly MA FY2024 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 302
actual_answer:
Beverly's capital plan looks ahead **ten years**. While the Beverly City Charter requires the Mayor to submit an annual capital improvement plan listing projects proposed over the next five years, the city intentionally extends its planning horizon beyond that minimum requirement. According to the FY2025 budget document, the plan includes projects that may require attention within the next ten years to help with long-term financial and service planning.

This longer timeframe allows the city to better anticipate future capital needs and make more informed decisions about priorities and affordability, even though the city recognizes that unexpected capital needs can arise at any time and assumptions about costs, timing, and funding sources may change.

question: How far ahead does Beverly's capital plan look?
expected_answer: The plan covers at least five years as required by the Beverly City Charter, but also includes projects that may need attention within the next ten years to support long-term financial and service planning.
source_title: Beverly MA FY2025 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 308
actual_answer:
Beverly's capital plan looks ahead **ten years**, though the City Charter requires the Mayor to submit a plan covering at least the next five years.

According to the FY2025 and FY2024 budget documents, the plan includes "a list of all capital improvements proposed to be undertaken over the next five years; however, it is important as a City to look beyond five years. Therefore, the plan includes projects that may require attention within the next ten years to help aid in long term financial and service planning."

However, it's worth noting that in June 2025, a City Council member raised concerns that the current capital plan "is largely a narrative document with estimated costs but lacks specificity about when projects will be funded or how they will be prioritized." He suggested the Council should work to improve the capital planning process to provide clearer information about funding timelines and project prioritization.
